data.title: Hims & Hers Q4 Preview: Is The Super Bowl Ad Worth $5B In Market Cap? (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

Hims & Hers Health (NYSE:HIMS) stock has been on a tear since the beginning of 2025.

With the stock exceeding my previous price target and up 212% since then, it has outperformed the S&P 500 by over 50x.

However, the recent price surge in February could be attributed to a combination of two factors that include 1) it's highly provocative and controversial Super Bowl ad titled “Sick of the System” and 2) the appointment of Robert F. Kennedy Jr. (“RFK”) to lead the Department of Health and Human Services, who investors believe is an ally to Hims & Hers Health.

With the company slated to report its Q4 FY24 earnings on February 24, the company is expected to grow its revenue and earnings per share by 90% and over 1000% YoY, respectively.

Over the last several quarters, the management has consistently raised their guidance, which has led to growing investor optimism. In fact, over the past three months, the stock has received several upgrades to its forward revenue and earnings estimates, even though it had fallen more than 25% in November after Amazon (AMZN) announced its plans to expand into hair loss and ED (erectile dysfunction) treatment sectors through its One Medical telehealth service.

At the moment, investor optimism in the stock is heavily focused on the potential growth rate of its compounded GLP-1 medications, which are roughly 85% cheaper than the branded versions. Plus, the appointment of RFK is expected to bring forth a lenient regulatory environment for compounded GLP-1s.

However, what is important to remember is that the growth story of Hims & Hers Health extends beyond its compounded GLP-1 medications, where subscribers excluding GLP-1 contributions still grew 40% YoY in Q3 FY24, with deepening adoption of its Personalized solutions across its specialties.

Unfortunately, with the latest surge in stock price, its market cap has climbed $5B since February 1. Therefore, one must ask the question whether the recent catalysts of the company's Super Bowl ad and RFK's appointment as Health Secretary are indeed worth the incremental $5B in its market cap appreciation since the beginning of the month.

I believe it would be prudent for both existing and new investors to hold off on adding positions at these levels and wait for management commentary in its upcoming Q4 earnings on the state of the overall landscape surrounding its Weight Loss segment, along with assessing potential competitive headwinds from Amazon.

As a result, I will rate the stock a “hold” at the moment with a price target of $58.

Pinning down the reason behind Hims & Hers recent stock price surge

Earlier in February, Hims & Hers Health rallied as it was set to debut its controversial Super Bowl ad, where it positioned itself as the “affordable, doctor-trusted, formulated in the US” solution to America’s obesity epidemic, while criticizing the US healthcare system for failing to take the required action.

Here’s an excerpt from its 60-second ad titled “Sick of the System”:

"This is America. Something's broken, and it's not our bodies.

It's the system.

Welcome to weight loss in America

A $160 billion industry that feeds on our failure.

There are medications that work, but they're priced for profits, not patients.”

In May 2024, Hims & Hers had announced access to compounded GLP-1 injections to its Weight Loss portfolio, and since then, a growing number of individuals have gotten access to these products, amid the ongoing shortage of semaglutide. At the same time, the management firmly believes that personalized dosages, breadth of treatment offerings, and frequent provider communication are all drivers of their success behind compounded GLP-1s, where a substantially higher percentage of users continue with their subscription at Week 4 and Week 12, compared to branded GLP-1 medications. Plus, they have continued to evolve their form factors to include oral options as well as drive accessible pricing through verticalization of operations, such as their acquisition of Medisource which is their first 503B compounding outsourcing facility.

For reference, Novo Nordisk’s (NVO) FDA-approved branded product, Wegovy, costs $1300-$2000 per month without insurance, while Hims’ compounded GLP-1 starts at $199 per month with an annual subscription, allowing them to democratize a level of care that has traditionally been available to a limited section of the population in the US.

This has enabled Hims & Hers Health to grow their total subscriber base utilizing a personalized solution at an accelerated pace, thus leading to the management consistently raising their revenue and earnings guidance.

However, the Super Bowl ad has stirred up significant controversy, with the Partnership for Safe Medicines calling the ad “dangerous” and urging the FDA to “enforce the laws and guidelines that protect Americans from misleading marketing in health products,” especially given that the ad leaves out any mention that its products are compounded, unapproved, and riskier for patients. At the same time, Pharma lobby group, The Pharmaceutical Research and Manufacturers of America also called the ad to be “a misleading Super Bowl ad that misrepresents their unapproved GLP-1 medicines.”

Despite the controversy, the stock soon climbed to reach an all-time high price of over $61 last week after RFK was confirmed to lead the Department of Health and Human Services. The basic idea behind the sudden surge in stock price is that RFK will turn out to be an ally for Hims & Hers Health, given that he is generally skeptical against vaccines and Big Pharma.

“Our sense (reading in-between the lines) is that the RFK-led HHS will take a fairly lenient stance with respect to the GLP compounders provided the drugs are seen as safe,” Mizuho strategist Jared Holz wrote, per Bloomberg. ”

This is how the company performed in Q3 FY24 earnings

Hims & Hers Health reported its Q3 FY24 earnings in early November, where it saw its revenue accelerate 77% YoY to $401.6M, while Adjusted EBITDA grew a whopping 317% YoY to $51.1M, with a margin expansion of 730 basis points to 12.3%.

During the quarter, subscribers on the Hims platform grew to 2.0M, with its subscriber base utilizing a Personalized solution growing 175% YoY to 1.03M. What I had also pointed out, that even excluding GLP-1 contribution, total subscribers grew 40% YoY as the company solidifies its positioning as a leader in personalized care, while continuing to drive improvements in its proprietary EMR (Electronic Medical Record) system that leverages structured data to help its providers deliver superior patient outcomes, particularly in its Mental Health and Weight Loss segments.

This has enabled the company to grow retention among users with personalized solutions across specialties with a 24% YoY increase in Monthly Online Revenue per Average Subscriber and a 48.5% increase in AOV (Average Order Value), thus unlocking incremental operating leverage on its path to achieving a 20% Adjusted EBITDA margin by 2030.

This is what to expect for Q4 FY24

With the company slated to report its Q4 FY24 earnings on February 24, the management had estimated its year-over-year revenue growth to continue accelerating between 89-91% YoY to $465-470M, with analysts projecting a revenue of roughly $469M for the quarter. I would also like to point out that over the last 90 days, the company has seen its forward revenue estimates for the coming years revise higher, as can be seen below.

From a profitability standpoint, the management expects an Adjusted EBITDA of $50-$55M for Q4 with a margin of 11-12%, with the full year Adjusted EBITDA estimated at roughly $1.463B, with a margin of 12%. Meanwhile, forward earnings per share estimates have been raised higher at a faster rate than total revenue, as can be seen below, partly explaining the stock’s outperformance as investors become confident of its path to a 20% Adjusted EBITDA margin by 2030.

But, before we move on, I would like to point out a few more things that I believe investors must pay attention to during the earnings call.

1. Forward Guidance, Contribution with and without GLP-1, Commentary on semaglutide shortage

While investors will be pleased with Hims beating its top and bottom lines, we need to pay attention to forward revenue and earnings guidance, especially given the last few quarters of raising guidance far higher than expected, which led analysts to revise up their forward growth estimates.

For instance, BofA analyst Allen Lutz believes that investors could be overestimating GLP-1-related revenue contribution, where he estimates core revenue growth of GLP-1 weight loss meds to decelerate from 47-50% in Q3 to 43% in Q4. Plus, there is added risk given that the FDA has determined Eli Lilly’s (LLY) shortage of its GLP-1 Zepbound to have been resolved.

As a result, investors need to be paying attention to management’s commentary on the state of the semaglutide shortage and therefore the share of revenue with and without its compounded GLP-1 meds.

Like I have said earlier, I don’t believe that compounded GLP-1 is the only tailwind in Hims’ growth story, where I expect subscriber count to grow across both its tenured and new specialties as it expands its personalized solutions to gain market share across different healthcare verticals. However, a demand normalization for its compounded GLP-1 may be accompanied by short-term stock price volatility.

2. Trend of Average Revenue per Subscriber and Average Order Value

What has boosted investor optimism in Hims is the growing trend of the above metrics as a result of growing retention among users with personalized solutions across specialties, thus enabling it to expand both its top and bottom lines at an accelerating pace.

For instance, these are some of the metrics I had shared in my previous post that indicate deepening adoption of Hims’ Personalized solutions among both its existing and new subscribers in Q3.

“The number of subscribers utilizing a personalized solution in Sexual health specialty has increased 3x.

80% of subscribers are using a personalized solution for both Men’s and Women’s Dermatology categories.

Meanwhile, the company is also seeing existing subscribers adopt Weight-Loss solutions, with 20% of Weight-Loss subscribers having a multi-specialty relationship with Hims & Hers Health and 15% of subscribers being treated for two or more conditions on the platform.”

A positive trend across both the metrics will be pivotal, as it will determine investors’ conviction in its long-term growth trajectory, as well as its viability to reach a 20% Adjusted EBITDA margin target by 2030.

3. Commentary on Amazon-related headwinds

Finally, in my previous post, I had discussed that Amazon will be expanding into the hair loss and ED treatment sectors through its One Medical telehealth service, with on-demand texting sessions and video visits priced at $29 and $49, respectively. Post the announcement, Hims’ stock had seen its share price drop over 25% in mid-November, given that hair loss and ED markets generate 80% gross margins for Hims & Hers Health.

However, I had reiterated my “buy” rating on the stock, claiming Hims & Hers has been distinguishing itself from competition by offering personalized solutions that provide users with access to customizable treatment solutions, which, I believed, could help it protect its retention rate.

In Q4, investors will be looking for management commentary on the potential impact from Amazon’s entrance into the space, which, I believe, will be important, as it will help us get a better idea of forward growth rates moving forward to reassess the company’s investment thesis.

Does the stock have any upside left?

While I had reduced my forward revenue and earnings estimates, given potential Amazon-related headwinds in my previous post, leading to a price target of $27.5. In my previous valuation model, I had estimated its FY24 revenue to grow at 59%, followed by a normalization to the high teens range. However, since the time of my publication, consensus estimates point to a revenue growth of 67% for FY24, followed by 42% in FY25 and 18% after that. Taking the analyst projections into consideration, Hims & Hers should generate roughly $2.44B in revenue by FY26.

From a profitability standpoint, I had earlier assumed profit margins to remain steady at around 11% until FY26, with little to no progress towards its FY30 Adjusted EBITDA margin of 20% as a result of Amazon related headwinds. However, assuming that Hims & Hers is able to combat competitive pressures with its superior personalization capabilities across specialties, it should be able to grow its margins to 15% as it makes incremental progress towards its 2030 targets. This will translate to an Adjusted EBITDA of $367M in FY26, which will be equivalent to a present value of $303 when discounted at 8%.

Assuming a price-to-earnings multiple of 42, which is roughly 3x the average multiple of the S&P 500 companies, given the superior growth rate of the company’s earnings during this period of time, we arrive at a price target of $58, which is roughly where the stock is currently trading at.

My final verdict and conclusions

There are a few things to keep in mind for existing investors and for those who are looking to add positions.

  1. The management can either guide higher or lower compared to analysts' forward projections in its upcoming earnings call. In the event it guides higher than consensus estimates, we should expect to see further multiple expansion from its current levels, leading the stock higher.

  2. However, should the company set guidance roughly at par with analysts' projections, or worse, lower, it may lead to a sharp selloff, especially since its latest rally has been driven by the hope that its compounded GLP-1 medications will contribute higher to its revenue growth from tailwinds that include an ongoing semaglutide shortage and a favorable regulation environment under RFK.

  3. At the same time, I would also like to reiterate (once again) that GLP-1 is just one part of its growth story and that I expect to see deepening adoption of its Personalized solutions across specialties for both existing and new subscribers in the coming years.

As an existing investor, I am hesitant to add incremental positions as the stock may be subject to tremendous bouts of volatility post earnings. As a result, I believe it is prudent to wait for management commentary to gain further clarity on the overall landscape surrounding its Weight loss segment and its contribution to the company’s top line, as well as potential competitive threats from Amazon.

data.title: AMD's AI Roadmap Is More Promising Than Most Realize
data.description:
Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (NASDAQ:AMD)(TSX:AMD:CA) is a large US semiconductor player known for its expertise in high-performance computing, graphics, and visualization tech. While it has always been perceived by the market as being overshadowed by Nvidia, which holds about 80% of the market share, AMD's data center business has been experiencing explosive growth, as per its latest financial results.

The company's MI300x chip series, in fact, demonstrates superior accelerator and chip performance to leading products in the market, according to AI experts. Moreover, its ROCm suite seems poised to become among the leading software stacks in the AI space.

While, at first glance, AMD appears to be quite pricey, its growth prospects more than justify its multiples, which, in my opinion, puts the stock clearly as being in the buy zone. For these reasons, I rate AMD as a buy.

AI-Driven Wins Contribute to Strong 2024 Performance

2024 was a strong year for AMD on many accounts, and its recent results proved that its fundamentals are as strong as ever. The company clearly demonstrated that it has made significant strides in capturing share in both the PC and server markets, driven by a launch of leadership products in each category.

Revenue for the full year climbed 14% year-over-year, taking the total to nearly $26 billion, raising the sales benchmark even higher this year. This is a notable feat following the 4% decline reported in 2023. The jump back to a double-digit take-off, driven mostly by the data center business bodes quite well for the company, as bears earlier pushed the idea that AMD's growth was mostly tied to the Covid-era boost in demand for computers, as the work-from-home phenomenon exploded. The company's data center AI business has undoubtedly been a major growth driver, with annual revenue from this segment alone surpassing $5 billion in 2024. That represents nearly one fifth of the company's total turnover.

I believe that, like most big players in tech, AMD too is seeing its growth narrative increasingly being defined by its deliberate pivot towards catering to the AI-related markets. For instance, AMD's Instinct accelerator lineup continues to gain rapid traction with some of the biggest tech companies involved in AI. Meta, for example, has been exclusively deploying MI300X accelerators for the meta.ai Llama 405B frontier model, while utilizing Instinct GPUs to its Grand Teton platform. Similarly, Microsoft has also been using MI300X to power several of its GPT-4 models for Copilot, while also scaling up its GPUs into the magnitude of thousands, for the purposes of both training and inference. This adoption by top players in the AI game indicates that AMD's optimized silicon enjoys strong demand in one of the most competitive spaces in tech, and is thus very capable of powering next gen generative models and high-performance computing workloads.

The MI300x chip series is, in my opinion, proving to be a real game-changer for AMD's AI standing, and in many respects, giving Nvidia's perceived market dominance a run for its money. It clearly surpasses the performance metrics of the H200 SXM by several key measures:

Similarly, while AMD's feats on the hardware side are quite impressive on their own, I am also glad to see that the company has not been complacent on the software side of the AI race, with its ROCm software stack significantly enhancing the ecosystem of its offerings.

The company has been very vocal about its ambitions to have the AMD ROCm suite become the industry's number one software stack for AI. At present, over a million ROCm models are online on Hugging Face, and can be executed immediately on AMD’s hardware without the need for any additional configuration or modifications. Moreover, its systems are fully integrated with top machine learning frameworks such as PyTorch and JAX, further facilitating widespread adoption.

Overall, AMD's progress on the AI front has me quite optimistic about its growth prospects. The company itself forecasts the data center accelerator space to achieve a total addressable market of half a trillion dollars by as early as 2028, growing at a CAGR of 60%. I believe AMD stands pretty well positioned to capture substantial share of the market moving forward, with a comprehensive focus on both the hardware side and its highly competitive software suite.

Valuation Assessment

Now let's get to the valuation part of our assessment.

At the time of the writing of this article, AMD's market cap is about $181 billion. Based on this, let's have a look at how its valuation metrics fare:

As we can see, going by most metrics, AMD appears to be more inflated than the tech sector in general. For instance, its $26 billion revenue gives it a trailing PS ratio of about 7, which is more than double the tech sector median PS ratio of 3.3. Similarly, its trailing (GAAP) PE ratio of 112 is particularly concerning, given that the median ratio in the tech sector is less than 33. Its trailing price to cash flow ratio of 60 is also nearly triple the IT sector median ratio of 21.

The only metric which seems to suggest some undervaluation is AMD's non-GAAP, forward PEG ratio of 0.55, which is quite lower than the tech sector median ratio of 1.9. This implies that the stock is undervalued considering its growth prospects.

Broadly speaking, it would seem that AMD is more pricey than the typical pick in the tech sector. However, I would like to point out that AMD is a much faster grower than the average player in tech, as is visible in the chart below:

One way to find out would be to assess the general relationship the market assigns different picks in the semiconductor space. To do so, I have created a scatter graph to highlight this relationship, and assess where AMD stands. The results can be seen below, where I have highlighted some of the most notable plays of the industry:

AMD's positioning above the line of best fit, in my opinion, is a good indication that the stock is clearly in the buy zone. Overall, AMD appears pricey at a first glance, but a look at its growth prospects, and its strong position in the AI data center space actually make it very attractive at its current pricing.

For me, this is a compelling indication that AMD is clearly undervalued, and therefore, I give the stock a buy rating.

Risk to Thesis

For the risk section of my analysis, I will elaborate on the one area that is on most people's minds. This relates, of course to the flat guidance on AI GPU sales in the first half of 2025. Given the monumental growth this side of AMD's business brought in 2024, this slowdown raises questions about the company's ability to sustain the explosive momentum it has just reported. This, of course, has triggered bears in the market to assume that AMD's extraordinary growth has arisen out of a one-off opportunity in the market, and thus should not be seen as a meaningful competitor to Nvidia.

While this pessimism may seem warranted, I do not assign it much weight for a number of reasons. Firstly, I don't believe that the slowdown reflects any underlying weakness in AMD's business itself, and rather points to a product-cycle timing issue. Over the long-term, I believe AMD's growth trajectory remains solid.

Moreover, the MI350 series is already showing strong early demand, due to which the company will initiate its production ramp earlier than previously anticipated. I believe that this accelerated timeline will give AMD a competitive edge, and a boost to its data center GPU momentum.

Overall, I am satisfied with AMD's efforts to better position itself to meet demand in a more sustained fashion, with the MI350 being a key driver.

Investor Takeaway

While, according to most market participants, Nvidia remains the dominant player in AI, I believe there is a strong case to be made that AMD is a lot more significant in this race than most give it credit for. While it may not necessarily surpass Nvidia in market share, in the short-term, its potential remains very attractive. Moreover, the DeepSeek phenomenon has equalized the playing field, in my opinion, which gives semiconductor companies like AMD a better fighting chance.

I rate AMD as a buy, given its strong AI opportunity, growth potential, and attractive pricing.
